---
title: "Omniscient Reader's Viewpoint (ORV)"
subtitle: " Advice for reading AI research papers and academic articles"
author: "Yuriel Ryan"
date: last-modified
---

## Introduction

Reading research papers is a skill --- it takes deliberate practice and good
habits. This guide compiles advice from graduate students and professors on
how to approach AI research papers and academic articles efficiently and critically.

::: {.callout-note}
This isn't a page that tells you "how to read a research paper". It's more of a "you can try these tips because it has worked for us" kind of page. Learning *when* to skim and
*when* to read deeply is part of the skill which you should apply to this page as well!
:::

---

## Before You Read

- **Know why you are reading.** Are you looking for related work, a specific
  method, or a broad survey of a field / problem (e.g., AI hallucinations)? Your goal determines how broad or deep you go.
- **Check the venue.** NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, EMNLP — knowing the conference
  gives you a sense of the paper's quality and focus area.
- **Read the abstract twice.** Once to understand the claim; once to identify
  what you should be looking for in the body.

---

## The Three-Pass Method

To start you off, the "three-pass method" is a well-known technique to become more efficient in your reading (some folks also refer to this as literature review).

### Pass 1 — The Bird's Eye View (5–10 min)

Read: title, abstract, section headings, figures and captions, conclusion.

**Goal:** Decide if the paper is worth a deeper read and form a rough hypothesis
about what it does.

### Pass 2 — Understanding the Contribution (30–60 min)

Read the body carefully, skipping proofs and implementation details.
Pay attention to figures, tables, and the experimental section.

**Goal:** Understand *what* the paper claims and *how* it was evaluated.

### Pass 3 — Critical Reconstruction (1–3 hours)

Read everything, including appendices and referenced papers.
Try to re-derive key results from scratch.

**Goal:** Deeply understand and critically evaluate the work.

---

## Critical Questions to Ask

- What is the core problem being solved?
- What is the key insight or novelty?
- What assumptions does the method rely on?
- How strong is the experimental validation?
- What are the limitations the authors acknowledge — and those they don't?

---

## Taking Notes

> *(Tips on note-taking strategies — to be added by contributors.)*

---

## Tips from Graduate Students

> *(Contributions welcome — share what has worked for you!)*

---

## Tips from Professors

> *(Contributions welcome.)*

---

## Resources

- [How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)](httyr.qmd) — EROP mentorship guide
- [SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)](sss-content.qmd) — academic writing tips
